question:
Let $Q_0(x) = 1$, $Q_1(x) = x$, and
\[
Q_n(x) = \frac{(Q_{n-1}(x))^2 - 1}{Q_{n-2}(x)}
\]
for all $n \geq 2$. Show that, whenever $n$ is a positive integer, $Q_n(x)$ is equal to a polynomial with integer coefficients.
solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Define $P_n(x)$ for $P_0(x) = 1$, $P_1(x) = x$, and $P_n(x) = x 
P_{n-1}(x)-P_{n-2}(x)$. We claim that $P_n(x) = Q_n(x)$ for all $n \geq 0$;
since $P_n(x)$ clearly is a polynomial 
with integer coefficients for all $n$, this will imply the desired result.

 Since $\{P_n\}$ and $\{Q_n\}$ are 
uniquely determined by their respective recurrence relations and the 
initial conditions $P_0,P_1$ or $Q_0,Q_1$, it suffices to check that 
$\{P_n\}$ satisfies the same recurrence as $Q$: that is, 
$(P_{n-1}(x))^2-P_n(x)P_{n-2}(x) = 1$ for all $n \geq 2$. Here is one 
proof of this: for $n \geq 1$, define the $2\times 2$ matrices 
\[
M_n = 
\begin{pmatrix} P_{n-1}(x) & P_n (x) \\ P_{n-2}(x) & 
P_{n-1}(x) \end{pmatrix}, \quad T = \begin{pmatrix} x & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
\]
with $P_{-1}(x) = 0$ (this value being consistent with the recurrence).
Then $\det(T) = 1$ and $T M_{n} = M_{n+1}$, so by induction on $n$ we have
\[
(P_{n-1}(x))^2-P_n(x)P_{n-2}(x) = \det(M_n) = 
\det(M_1) = 1.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
A similar argument shows that any second-order linear recurrent sequence also satisfies a quadratic second-order recurrence relation.
A familiar example is the identity $F_{n-1} F_{n+1} - F_n^2 = (-1)^{n}$ for $F_n$ the $n$-th Fibonacci number. 
More examples come from various classes of \emph{orthogonal polynomials}, including the Chebyshev polynomials mentioned below.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
We establish directly that $Q_n(x) = x 
Q_{n-1}(x)-Q_{n-2}(x)$, which again suffices. 
From the equation
\[
1 = Q_{n-1}(x)^2 - Q_n(x) Q_{n-2}(x) = Q_n(x)^2 - Q_{n+1}(x) Q_{n-1}(x)
\]
we deduce that
\[
Q_{n-1}(x)(Q_{n-1}(x) + Q_{n+1}(x)) = Q_n(x) (Q_n(x) + Q_{n-2}(x)).
\]
Since $\deg(Q_n(x)) = n$ by an obvious induction, the polynomials $Q_n(x)$ are all nonzero. We may thus rewrite the previous equation as
\[
\frac{Q_{n+1}(x) + Q_{n-1}(x)}{Q_n(x)} = \frac{Q_n(x) + Q_{n-2}(x)}{Q_{n-1}(x)},
\]
meaning that the rational functions $\frac{Q_n(x) + Q_{n-2}(x)}{Q_{n-1}(x)}$
are all equal to a constant value. By taking $n=2$ and computing from the definition that $Q_2(x) = x^2-1$,
we find the constant value to be $x$; this yields the desired recurrence.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By induction, one may also obtain the explicit formula
\[
Q_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} (-1)^k \binom{n-k}{k} x^{n-2k}.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
In light of the explicit formula for $Q_n(x)$,
Karl Mahlburg suggests the following bijective interpretation of the identity
$Q_{n-1}(x)^2 - Q_n(x) Q_{n-2}(x) = 1$.
Consider the set $C_n$ of integer compositions of $n$ with all parts 1 or 2; 
these are ordered tuples $(c_1, \dots, c_k)$ such that $c_1 + \cdots + c_k = n$ and $c_i  \in \{1,2\}$ for all $i$.
For a given composition $c$, let $o(c)$ and $d(c)$ denote the number of 1's and 2's, respectively.
Define the generating function
\[
R_n(x) = \sum_{c \in C_n} x^{o(c)};
\]
then $R_n(x) = \sum_{j} \binom{n-j}{j} x^{n-2j}$, so that $Q_n(x) = i^{-n/2} R_n(ix)$.
(The polynomials $R_n(x)$ are sometimes called \emph{Fibonacci polynomials}; they satisfy $R_n(1) = F_n$.
This interpretation of $F_n$ as the cardinality of $C_n$ first arose in the study of Sanskrit prosody, specifically the analysis of a line of verse as a sequence of long and short syllables, at least 500 years prior to
the work of Fibonacci.)

The original identity is equivalent to the identity
\[
R_{n+1}(x) R_{n-1}(x) - R_n(x)^2 = (-1)^{n-1}.
\]
This follows because if we identify the composition $c$ with a tiling of a $1 \times n$ rectangle by $1 \times 1$ squares and $1 \times 2$ dominoes, it is \emph{almost} a bijection to place two tilings of length $n$ on top of each other, offset by one square, and hinge at the first possible point (which is the first square in either). This only fails when both tilings are all dominoes, which gives the term $(-1)^{n-1}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem appeared on the 2012 India National Math Olympiad; see
\url{https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c6h1219629}.
Another problem based on the same idea is problem A2 from the 1993 Putnam.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $quotpoly_0(variablex) = 1$, $quotpoly_1(variablex) = variablex$, and
\[
quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) = \frac{(quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex))^2 - 1}{quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex)}
\]
for all $indexn \geq 2$. Show that, whenever $indexn$ is a positive integer, $quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)$ is equal to a polynomial with integer coefficients.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Define $polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex)$ for $polyseqpn_0(variablex) = 1$, $polyseqpn_1(variablex) = variablex$, and $polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex) = variablex 
polyseqpn_{indexn-1}(variablex)-polyseqpn_{indexn-2}(variablex)$. We claim that $polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex) = quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)$ for all $indexn \geq 0$;
since $polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex)$ clearly is a polynomial 
with integer coefficients for all $indexn$, this will imply the desired result.

 Since $\{polyseqpn_{indexn}\}$ and $\{quotpoly_{indexn}\}$ are 
uniquely determined by their respective recurrence relations and the 
initial conditions $polyseqpn_0,polyseqpn_1$ or $quotpoly_0,quotpoly_1$, it suffices to check that 
$\{polyseqpn_{indexn}\}$ satisfies the same recurrence as $Q$: that is, 
$(polyseqpn_{indexn-1}(variablex))^2-polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex)polyseqpn_{indexn-2}(variablex) = 1$ for all $indexn \geq 2$. Here is one 
proof of this: for $indexn \geq 1$, define the $2\times 2$ matrices 
\[
matrixmn_{indexn} = 
\begin{pmatrix} polyseqpn_{indexn-1}(variablex) & polyseqpn_{indexn} (variablex) \\ polyseqpn_{indexn-2}(variablex) & 
polyseqpn_{indexn-1}(variablex) \end{pmatrix}, \quad transmat = \begin{pmatrix} variablex & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
\]
with $polyseqpn_{-1}(variablex) = 0$ (this value being consistent with the recurrence).
Then $\det(transmat) = 1$ and $transmat\, matrixmn_{indexn} = matrixmn_{indexn+1}$, so by induction on $indexn$ we have
\[
(polyseqpn_{indexn-1}(variablex))^2-polyseqpn_{indexn}(variablex)polyseqpn_{indexn-2}(variablex) = \det(matrixmn_{indexn}) = 
\det(matrixmn_{1}) = 1.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
A similar argument shows that any second-order linear recurrent sequence also satisfies a quadratic second-order recurrence relation.
A familiar example is the identity $fibnumber_{indexn-1} fibnumber_{indexn+1} - fibnumber_{indexn}^2 = (-1)^{indexn}$ for $fibnumber_{indexn}$ the $indexn$-th Fibonacci number. 
More examples come from various classes of \emph{orthogonal polynomials}, including the Chebyshev polynomials mentioned below.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
We establish directly that $quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) = variablex 
quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)-quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex)$, which again suffices. 
From the equation
\[
1 = quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)^2 - quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex) = quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)^2 - quotpoly_{indexn+1}(variablex) quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)
\]
we deduce that
\[
quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)(quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex) + quotpoly_{indexn+1}(variablex)) = quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) (quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) + quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex)).
\]
Since $\deg(quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)) = indexn$ by an obvious induction, the polynomials $quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)$ are all nonzero. We may thus rewrite the previous equation as
\[
\frac{quotpoly_{indexn+1}(variablex) + quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)}{quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)} = \frac{quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) + quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex)}{quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)},
\]
meaning that the rational functions $\frac{quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) + quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex)}{quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)}$
are all equal to a constant value. By taking $indexn=2$ and computing from the definition that $quotpoly_{2}(variablex) = variablex^2-1$,
we find the constant value to be $variablex$; this yields the desired recurrence.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By induction, one may also obtain the explicit formula
\[
quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) = \sum_{counterk=0}^{\lfloor indexn/2 \rfloor} (-1)^{counterk} \binom{indexn-counterk}{counterk} variablex^{indexn-2counterk}.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
In light of the explicit formula for $quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex)$,
Karl Mahlburg suggests the following bijective interpretation of the identity
$quotpoly_{indexn-1}(variablex)^2 - quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) quotpoly_{indexn-2}(variablex) = 1$.
Consider the set $compsetn_{indexn}$ of integer compositions of $indexn$ with all parts 1 or 2; 
these are ordered tuples $(singlec_1, \dots, singlec_{counterk})$ such that $singlec_1 + \cdots + singlec_{counterk} = indexn$ and $singleci  \in \{1,2\}$ for all $i$.
For a given composition $singlec$, let $o(singlec)$ and $d(singlec)$ denote the number of 1's and 2's, respectively.
Define the generating function
\[
fibpolyn_{indexn}(variablex) = \sum_{singlec \in compsetn_{indexn}} variablex^{o(singlec)};
\]
then $fibpolyn_{indexn}(variablex) = \sum_{counterj} \binom{indexn-counterj}{counterj} variablex^{indexn-2counterj}$, so that $quotpoly_{indexn}(variablex) = i^{-indexn/2} fibpolyn_{indexn}(i\,variablex)$.
(The polynomials $fibpolyn_{indexn}(variablex)$ are sometimes called \emph{Fibonacci polynomials}; they satisfy $fibpolyn_{indexn}(1) = fibnumber_{indexn}$.
This interpretation of $fibnumber_{indexn}$ as the cardinality of $compsetn_{indexn}$ first arose in the study of Sanskrit prosody, specifically the analysis of a line of verse as a sequence of long and short syllables, at least 500 years prior to
the work of Fibonacci.)

The original identity is equivalent to the identity
\[
fibpolyn_{indexn+1}(variablex) fibpolyn_{indexn-1}(variablex) - fibpolyn_{indexn}(variablex)^2 = (-1)^{indexn-1}.
\]
This follows because if we identify the composition $singlec$ with a tiling of a $1 \times indexn$ rectangle by $1 \times 1$ squares and $1 \times 2$ dominoes, it is \emph{almost} a bijection to place two tilings of length $indexn$ on top of each other, offset by one square, and hinge at the first possible point (which is the first square in either). This only fails when both tilings are all dominoes, which gives the term $(-1)^{indexn-1}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem appeared on the 2012 India National Math Olympiad; see
\url{https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c6h1219629}.
Another problem based on the same idea is problem A2 from the 1993 Putnam.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $Q_0(celestial) = 1$, $Q_1(celestial) = celestial$, and
\[
Q_{pineapple}(celestial) = \frac{(Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial))^2 - 1}{Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial)}
\]
for all $pineapple \geq 2$. Show that, whenever $pineapple$ is a positive integer, $Q_{pineapple}(celestial)$ is equal to a polynomial with integer coefficients.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Define $nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial)$ for $nightingale_0(celestial) = 1$, $nightingale_1(celestial) = celestial$, and $nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial) = celestial 
nightingale_{pineapple-1}(celestial)-nightingale_{pineapple-2}(celestial)$. We claim that $nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial) = waterfall_{pineapple}(celestial)$ for all $pineapple \geq 0$; since $nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial)$ clearly is a polynomial 
with integer coefficients for all $pineapple$, this will imply the desired result.

Since $\{nightingale_{pineapple}\}$ and $\{waterfall_{pineapple}\}$ are 
uniquely determined by their respective recurrence relations and the 
initial conditions $nightingale_0,nightingale_1$ or $Q_0,Q_1$, it suffices to check that 
$\{nightingale_{pineapple}\}$ satisfies the same recurrence as $Q$: that is, 
$(nightingale_{pineapple-1}(celestial))^2-nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial)nightingale_{pineapple-2}(celestial) = 1$ for all $pineapple \geq 2$. Here is one 
proof of this: for $pineapple \geq 1$, define the $2\times 2$ matrices 
\[
semaphore_{pineapple} = 
\begin{pmatrix} nightingale_{pineapple-1}(celestial) & nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial) \\ nightingale_{pineapple-2}(celestial) & 
nightingale_{pineapple-1}(celestial) \end{pmatrix}, \quad moonlight = \begin{pmatrix} celestial & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
\]
with $nightingale_{-1}(celestial) = 0$ (this value being consistent with the recurrence).
Then $\det(moonlight) = 1$ and $moonlight\,semaphore_{pineapple} = semaphore_{pineapple+1}$, so by induction on $pineapple$ we have
\[
(nightingale_{pineapple-1}(celestial))^2-nightingale_{pineapple}(celestial)nightingale_{pineapple-2}(celestial) = \det(semaphore_{pineapple}) = 
\det(semaphore_1) = 1.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
A similar argument shows that any second-order linear recurrent sequence also satisfies a quadratic second-order recurrence relation.
A familiar example is the identity $F_{pineapple-1} F_{pineapple+1} - F_{pineapple}^2 = (-1)^{pineapple}$ for $F_{pineapple}$ the $pineapple$-th Fibonacci number. 
More examples come from various classes of \emph{orthogonal polynomials}, including the Chebyshev polynomials mentioned below.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
We establish directly that $Q_{pineapple}(celestial) = celestial 
Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)-Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial)$, which again suffices. 
From the equation
\[
1 = Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)^2 - Q_{pineapple}(celestial) Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial) = Q_{pineapple}(celestial)^2 - Q_{pineapple+1}(celestial) Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)
\]
we deduce that
\[
Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)(Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial) + Q_{pineapple+1}(celestial)) = Q_{pineapple}(celestial) (Q_{pineapple}(celestial) + Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial)).
\]
Since $\deg(Q_{pineapple}(celestial)) = pineapple$ by an obvious induction, the polynomials $Q_{pineapple}(celestial)$ are all nonzero. We may thus rewrite the previous equation as
\[
\frac{Q_{pineapple+1}(celestial) + Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)}{Q_{pineapple}(celestial)} = \frac{Q_{pineapple}(celestial) + Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial)}{Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)},
\]
meaning that the rational functions $\frac{Q_{pineapple}(celestial) + Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial)}{Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)}$
are all equal to a constant value. By taking $pineapple=2$ and computing from the definition that $Q_2(celestial) = celestial^2-1$,
we find the constant value to be $celestial$; this yields the desired recurrence.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By induction, one may also obtain the explicit formula
\[
Q_{pineapple}(celestial) = \sum_{toothpick=0}^{\lfloor pineapple/2 \rfloor} (-1)^{toothpick} \binom{pineapple-toothpick}{toothpick} celestial^{pineapple-2\,toothpick}.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
In light of the explicit formula for $Q_{pineapple}(celestial)$,
Karl Mahlburg suggests the following bijective interpretation of the identity
$Q_{pineapple-1}(celestial)^2 - Q_{pineapple}(celestial) Q_{pineapple-2}(celestial) = 1$.
Consider the set $cartwheel$ of integer compositions of $pineapple$ with all parts 1 or 2; 
these are ordered tuples $(watermelon_1, \dots, watermelon_{toothpick})$ such that $watermelon_1 + \cdots + watermelon_{toothpick} = pineapple$ and $rainforest  \in \{1,2\}$ for all $i$.
For a given composition $watermelon$, let $o(watermelon)$ and $d(watermelon)$ denote the number of 1's and 2's, respectively.
Define the generating function
\[
hedgehog_{pineapple}(celestial) = \sum_{watermelon \in cartwheel} celestial^{o(watermelon)};
\]
then $hedgehog_{pineapple}(celestial) = \sum_{philosopher} \binom{pineapple-philosopher}{philosopher} celestial^{pineapple-2\,philosopher}$, so that $Q_{pineapple}(celestial) = i^{-pineapple/2} hedgehog_{pineapple}(i celestial)$.
(The polynomials $hedgehog_{pineapple}(celestial)$ are sometimes called \emph{Fibonacci polynomials}; they satisfy $hedgehog_{pineapple}(1) = butterfly$.
This interpretation of $butterfly$ as the cardinality of $cartwheel$ first arose in the study of Sanskrit prosody, specifically the analysis of a line of verse as a sequence of long and short syllables, at least 500 years prior to
the work of Fibonacci.)

The original identity is equivalent to the identity
\[
hedgehog_{pineapple+1}(celestial) hedgehog_{pineapple-1}(celestial) - hedgehog_{pineapple}(celestial)^2 = (-1)^{pineapple-1}.
\]
This follows because if we identify the composition $watermelon$ with a tiling of a $1 \times pineapple$ rectangle by $1 \times 1$ squares and $1 \times 2$ dominoes, it is \emph{almost} a bijection to place two tilings of length $pineapple$ on top of each other, offset by one square, and hinge at the first possible point (which is the first square in either). This only fails when both tilings are all dominoes, which gives the term $(-1)^{pineapple-1}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem appeared on the 2012 India National Math Olympiad; see
\url{https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c6h1219629}.
Another problem based on the same idea is problem A2 from the 1993 Putnam.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $antipolynomial_0(constant) = 1$, $antipolynomial_1(constant) = constant$, and
\[
antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = \frac{(antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant))^2 - 1}{antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)}
\]
for all $\infinity \geq 2$. Show that, whenever $\infinity$ is a positive integer, $antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$ is equal to a polynomial with integer coefficients.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Define $nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$ for $nonpolynomial_0(constant) = 1$, $nonpolynomial_1(constant) = constant$, and $nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = constant \
nonpolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)-nonpolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)$. We claim that $nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$ for all $\infinity \geq 0$;
since $nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$ clearly is a polynomial 
with integer coefficients for all $\infinity$, this will imply the desired result.

 Since $\{nonpolynomial_{\infinity}\}$ and $\{antipolynomial_{\infinity}\}$ are 
uniquely determined by their respective recurrence relations and the 
initial conditions $nonpolynomial_0,nonpolynomial_1$ or $antipolynomial_0,antipolynomial_1$, it suffices to check that 
$\{nonpolynomial_{\infinity}\}$ satisfies the same recurrence as $antipolynomial$: that is, 
$(nonpolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant))^2-nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)nonpolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant) = 1$ for all $\infinity \geq 2$. Here is one 
proof of this: for $\infinity \geq 1$, define the $2\times 2$ matrices 
\[
unmatrix_{\infinity} = 
\begin{pmatrix} nonpolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant) & nonpolynomial_{\infinity} (constant) \\ nonpolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant) & 
nonpolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant) \end{pmatrix}, \quad scalarzero = \begin{pmatrix} constant & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
\]
with $nonpolynomial_{-1}(constant) = 0$ (this value being consistent with the recurrence).
Then $\det(scalarzero) = 1$ and $scalarzero \unmatrix_{\infinity} = \unmatrix_{\infinity+1}$, so by induction on $\infinity$ we have
\[
(nonpolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant))^2-nonpolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)nonpolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant) = \det(\unmatrix_{\infinity}) = 
\det(\unmatrix_1) = 1.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
A similar argument shows that any second-order linear recurrent sequence also satisfies a quadratic second-order recurrence relation.
A familiar example is the identity $defibrillator_{\infinity-1} defibrillator_{\infinity+1} - defibrillator_{\infinity}^2 = (-1)^{\infinity}$ for $defibrillator_{\infinity}$ the $\infinity$-th Fibonacci number. 
More examples come from various classes of \emph{orthogonal polynomials}, including the Chebyshev polynomials mentioned below.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
We establish directly that $antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = constant 
antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)-antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)$, which again suffices. 
From the equation
\[
1 = antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)^2 - antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant) = antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)^2 - antipolynomial_{\infinity+1}(constant) antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)
\]
we deduce that
\[
antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)(antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant) + antipolynomial_{\infinity+1}(constant)) = antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) (antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) + antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)).
\]
Since $\deg(antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)) = \infinity$ by an obvious induction, the polynomials $antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$ are all nonzero. We may thus rewrite the previous equation as
\[
\frac{antipolynomial_{\infinity+1}(constant) + antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)}{antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)} = \frac{antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) + antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)}{antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)},
\]
meaning that the rational functions $\frac{antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) + antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant)}{antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)}$
are all equal to a constant value. By taking $\infinity=2$ and computing from the definition that $antipolynomial_2(constant) = constant^2-1$,
we find the constant value to be $constant$; this yields the desired recurrence.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By induction, one may also obtain the explicit formula
\[
antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = \sum_{terminator=0}^{\lfloor \infinity/2 \rfloor} (-1)^{terminator} \binom{\infinity-terminator}{terminator} constant^{\infinity-2terminator}.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
In light of the explicit formula for $antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant)$,
Karl Mahlburg suggests the following bijective interpretation of the identity
$antipolynomial_{\infinity-1}(constant)^2 - antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) antipolynomial_{\infinity-2}(constant) = 1$.
Consider the set $disassembly_{\infinity}$ of integer compositions of $\infinity$ with all parts 1 or 2; 
these are ordered tuples $(chaosness_1, \dots, chaosness_{terminator})$ such that $chaosness_1 + \cdots + chaosness_{terminator} = \infinity$ and $chaosnessi  \in \{1,2\}$ for all $i$.
For a given composition $chaosness$, let $o(chaosness)$ and $d(chaosness)$ denote the number of 1's and 2's, respectively.
Define the generating function
\[
antifibonacci_{\infinity}(constant) = \sum_{chaosness \in disassembly_{\infinity}} constant^{o(chaosness)};
\]
then $antifibonacci_{\infinity}(constant) = \sum_{finishline} \binom{\infinity-finishline}{finishline} constant^{\infinity-2finishline}$, so that $antipolynomial_{\infinity}(constant) = i^{-\infinity/2} antifibonacci_{\infinity}(i constant)$.
(The polynomials $antifibonacci_{\infinity}(constant)$ are sometimes called \emph{Fibonacci polynomials}; they satisfy $antifibonacci_{\infinity}(1) = defibrillator_{\infinity}$.
This interpretation of $defibrillator_{\infinity}$ as the cardinality of $disassembly_{\infinity}$ first arose in the study of Sanskrit prosody, specifically the analysis of a line of verse as a sequence of long and short syllables, at least 500 years prior to
the work of Fibonacci.)

The original identity is equivalent to the identity
\[
antifibonacci_{\infinity+1}(constant) antifibonacci_{\infinity-1}(constant) - antifibonacci_{\infinity}(constant)^2 = (-1)^{\infinity-1}.
\]
This follows because if we identify the composition $chaosness$ with a tiling of a $1 \times \infinity$ rectangle by $1 \times 1$ squares and $1 \times 2$ dominoes, it is \emph{almost} a bijection to place two tilings of length $\infinity$ on top of each other, offset by one square, and hinge at the first possible point (which is the first square in either). This only fails when both tilings are all dominoes, which gives the term $(-1)^{\infinity-1}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem appeared on the 2012 India National Math Olympiad; see
\url{https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c6h1219629}.
Another problem based on the same idea is problem A2 from the 1993 Putnam.

garbled_string.question:
Let $Q_0(yqfdslrm) = 1$, $Q_1(yqfdslrm) = yqfdslrm$, and
\[
Q_{vajkebtu}(yqfdslrm) = \frac{(Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm))^2 - 1}{Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)}
\]
for all $vajkebtu \geq 2$. Show that, whenever $vajkebtu$ is a positive integer, $rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)$ is equal to a polynomial with integer coefficients.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Define $ufvadzor(yqfdslrm)$ for $P_0(yqfdslrm) = 1$, $P_1(yqfdslrm) = yqfdslrm$, and $ufvadzor(yqfdslrm) = yqfdslrm 
P_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)-P_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)$. We claim that $ufvadzor(yqfdslrm) = rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)$ for all $vajkebtu \geq 0$;
since $ufvadzor(yqfdslrm)$ clearly is a polynomial 
with integer coefficients for all $vajkebtu$, this will imply the desired result.

 Since $\{ufvadzor\}$ and $\{rdlixqma\}$ are 
uniquely determined by their respective recurrence relations and the 
initial conditions $P_0,P_1$ or $Q_0,Q_1$, it suffices to check that 
$\{ufvadzor\}$ satisfies the same recurrence as $Q$: that is, 
$(P_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm))^2-ufvadzor(yqfdslrm)P_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm) = 1$ for all $vajkebtu \geq 2$. Here is one 
proof of this: for $vajkebtu \geq 1$, define the $2\times 2$ matrices 
\[
sgwhxelo = 
\begin{pmatrix} P_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm) & ufvadzor (yqfdslrm) \\ P_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm) & 
P_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm) \end{pmatrix}, \quad kmvjoqre = \begin{pmatrix} yqfdslrm & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
\]
with $P_{-1}(yqfdslrm) = 0$ (this value being consistent with the recurrence).
Then $\det(kmvjoqre) = 1$ and $kmvjoqre sgwhxelo = M_{vajkebtu+1}$, so by induction on $vajkebtu$ we have
\[
(P_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm))^2-ufvadzor(yqfdslrm)P_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm) = \det(sgwhxelo) = 
\det(M_1) = 1.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
A similar argument shows that any second-order linear recurrent sequence also satisfies a quadratic second-order recurrence relation.
A familiar example is the identity $F_{n-1} F_{n+1} - F_n^2 = (-1)^{n}$ for $F_n$ the $n$-th Fibonacci number. 
More examples come from various classes of \emph{orthogonal polynomials}, including the Chebyshev polynomials mentioned below.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
We establish directly that $rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) = yqfdslrm 
Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)-Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)$, which again suffices. 
From the equation
\[
1 = Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)^2 - rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm) = rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)^2 - Q_{vajkebtu+1}(yqfdslrm) Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)
\]
we deduce that
\[
Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)(Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm) + Q_{vajkebtu+1}(yqfdslrm)) = rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) (rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) + Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)).
\]
Since $\deg(rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)) = vajkebtu$ by an obvious induction, the polynomials $rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)$ are all nonzero. We may thus rewrite the previous equation as
\[
\frac{Q_{vajkebtu+1}(yqfdslrm) + Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)}{rdlixqma(yqfdslrm)} = \frac{rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) + Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)}{Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)},
\]
meaning that the rational functions $\frac{rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) + Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm)}{Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)}$
are all equal to a constant value. By taking $vajkebtu=2$ and computing from the definition that $Q_2(yqfdslrm) = yqfdslrm^2-1$,
we find the constant value to be $yqfdslrm$; this yields the desired recurrence.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By induction, one may also obtain the explicit formula
\[
rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) = \sum_{plodmfse=0}^{\lfloor vajkebtu/2 \rfloor} (-1)^{plodmfse} \binom{vajkebtu-plodmfse}{plodmfse} yqfdslrm^{vajkebtu-2plodmfse}.
\]

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
In light of the explicit formula for rdlixqma(yqfdslrm),
Karl Mahlburg suggests the following bijective interpretation of the identity
$Q_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm)^2 - rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) Q_{vajkebtu-2}(yqfdslrm) = 1$.
Consider the set $bwcerhla$ of integer compositions of $vajkebtu$ with all parts 1 or 2; 
these are ordered tuples $(qzxwvtnp_1, \dots, qzxwvtnp_{plodmfse})$ such that $qzxwvtnp_1 + \cdots + qzxwvtnp_{plodmfse} = vajkebtu$ and $hjgrksla  \in \{1,2\}$ for all $i$.
For a given composition $qzxwvtnp$, let $o(qzxwvtnp)$ and $d(qzxwvtnp)$ denote the number of 1's and 2's, respectively.
Define the generating function
\[
twxzspau(yqfdslrm) = \sum_{qzxwvtnp \in bwcerhla} yqfdslrm^{o(qzxwvtnp)};
\]
then $twxzspau(yqfdslrm) = \sum_{nrktuvgh} \binom{vajkebtu-nrktuvgh}{nrktuvgh} yqfdslrm^{vajkebtu-2nrktuvgh}$, so that $rdlixqma(yqfdslrm) = i^{-vajkebtu/2} twxzspau(i yqfdslrm)$.
(The polynomials $twxzspau(yqfdslrm)$ are sometimes called \emph{Fibonacci polynomials}; they satisfy $twxzspau(1) = zoqmktps$.
This interpretation of $zoqmktps$ as the cardinality of $bwcerhla$ first arose in the study of Sanskrit prosody, specifically the analysis of a line of verse as a sequence of long and short syllables, at least 500 years prior to
the work of Fibonacci.)

The original identity is equivalent to the identity
\[
R_{vajkebtu+1}(yqfdslrm) R_{vajkebtu-1}(yqfdslrm) - R_{vajkebtu}(yqfdslrm)^2 = (-1)^{vajkebtu-1}.
\]
This follows because if we identify the composition $qzxwvtnp$ with a tiling of a $1 \times vajkebtu$ rectangle by $1 \times 1$ squares and $1 \times 2$ dominoes, it is \emph{almost} a bijection to place two tilings of length $vajkebtu$ on top of each other, offset by one square, and hinge at the first possible point (which is the first square in either). This only fails when both tilings are all dominoes, which gives the term $(-1)^{vajkebtu-1}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem appeared on the 2012 India National Math Olympiad; see
\url{https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c6h1219629}.
Another problem based on the same idea is problem A2 from the 1993 Putnam.

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
Q_0(x)=1,\qquad Q_1(x)=x,\qquad 
Q_n(x)=\frac{\bigl(Q_{n-1}(x)\bigr)^2-(-1)^{n-1}}{\,Q_{n-2}(x)}\qquad(n\ge 2).
\]
Show that every $Q_n(x)$ is an integer-coefficient polynomial.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Define the auxiliary sequence of polynomials

    P_0(x)=1,  P_1(x)=x,
    P_n(x)=x\cdot P_{n-1}(x)+P_{n-2}(x)

for n\geq 2.  Clearly this is a linear recurrence with integer-polynomial coefficients, so each P_n(x)\in \mathbb{Z}[x].

Step 2.  Form the matrices

    M_n = [ P_{n-1}(x)   P_n(x)  
           P_{n-2}(x)   P_{n-1}(x) ],  
    T   = [   x       1     
              1       0   ].

One checks at once that M_{n+1} = T\cdot M_n, and det T = x\cdot 0-1\cdot 1 = -1.

Step 3.  Since P_{-1} := x\cdot P_0-P_1 = 0, we have

    M_1 = [ 1   x  
            0   1 ],

so det M_1=1.  Hence by induction det M_n = (-1)^{n-1} for all n\geq 1.  On the other hand

    det M_n = P_{n-1}^2 - P_n\cdot P_{n-2},

so

    P_{n-1}(x)^2 - P_n(x)\cdot P_{n-2}(x) = (-1)^{n-1}.

Step 4.  Rearranging gives exactly

    P_n(x) = [P_{n-1}(x)^2 - (-1)^{n-1}]/P_{n-2}(x),

which is the defining recurrence for Q_n(x).  Since P_0=Q_0 and P_1=Q_1, uniqueness of such a second-order recurrence yields P_n\equiv Q_n.

Step 5.  Each P_n(x) lies in \mathbb{Z}[x], hence so does Q_n(x).  This completes the proof that every Q_n(x) is an integer-coefficient polynomial.

core_steps: Introduce a second–order linear sequence Pₙ with integer coefficients (P₀=1, P₁=x,  Pₙ = xPₙ₋₁ − Pₙ₋₂).
Package (Pₙ₋₂,Pₙ₋₁,Pₙ) into 2×2 matrices Mₙ and note Mₙ₊₁ = T·Mₙ with det T = 1.
From det Mₙ = det M₁ = 1 deduce the quadratic invariant (Pₙ₋₁)² − PₙPₙ₋₂ = 1 for every n ≥ 2.
Observe that this invariant is exactly the recurrence that defines Qₙ, so the two sequences coincide by the shared initial data.
Since Pₙ obeys a linear recurrence with integer coefficients, every Qₙ is an integer-coefficient polynomial.

slot1.description: Constant appearing in the quadratic invariant and in the numerator of Qₙ’s definition; equal to det M₁.

slot2.description: Sign/scale on the Pₙ₋₂ term in the linear recurrence (equivalently, the upper-right entry of T).  Changing −1 to any non-zero integer c still yields the same argument with det T = −c.
